content: You open the fridge. Stare for 45 seconds. Close it. Open it again. Nothing changed. Order DoorDash. This is the meal planning crisis nobody talks about.

Real question: when you don't know what to make for dinner, how often do you actually have food at home?

🔵 Always — full fridge, zero ideas
🟣 Usually — something's there
⚪ Rarely — genuinely empty
🟡 I meal plan like an adult (respect)

your tenant doesn't care that it's sunday. the pipes don't either.
being a small landlord is just being on call indefinitely with no HR department

maintenance request management for landlords with 1-3 units:

- text thread with no context
- voicemail you'll "listen to later"
- sticky note that fell behind the desk
- vibes

we deserve better systems than this
Building a startup from Namibia means my "office" is wherever the wifi holds. Right now: fixing a bug where the AI suggests "pasta carbonara" when you only have spaghetti and regret.

The real problem isn't that people can't cook. It's that deciding *what* to cook with what you have takes more mental energy than the cooking itself. Decision fatigue hits before you even touch the stove.

if you're a small landlord, start documenting maintenance requests with timestamps the moment you get them

not because you expect a dispute
but because the one time you don't, that's when you need it

(ask me how I know)

Built pantrymate.net for this exact problem. Type what's in your fridge → get meals you can actually make. Takes 30 seconds. Free to try. No account needed to start.

It's not magic. It's just a faster brain for your pantry.

built a simple fix for this:

each rental unit gets its own URL → tenants submit requests without making an account → you see everything in one dashboard

called UnitFix. free for 1 unit, $29/mo for up to 5.
unitfix.app

for landlords who are tired of managing maintenance in their texts

Week 3 of solo founder life update: added meal history so you stop getting suggested the same 3 dishes. Also: Google Play launch incoming. Android users, you're next.

MRR: still embarrassingly small. But it's growing. 🧪

stages of a small landlord's career:

1. "how hard can it be"
2. first maintenance call at 11pm
3. buying a label maker for keys
4. googling "how to respond to maintenance requests professionally"
5. setting up actual systems
6. peace (temporary)

What's the most random thing you've cooked because you refused to go to the grocery store?

I'll start: tuna + leftover rice + whatever that sauce was. Ate it. 0 regrets.

the #1 thing that protects you in a tenant dispute isn't being a good landlord

it's being a documented landlord

timestamped records of every request, every response, every repair

this is unsexy until the moment it isn't

ok so how do property managers stay sane

I used to throw out $20–$40 of food a week. Not because I didn't care, just because by the time I figured out what to cook, I'd already ordered something.

Started doing this instead: before I grocery shop, I type everything I still have into PantryMate (pantrymate.net). It tells me what I can actually cook with what's already there. Usually I can get 2–3 more dinners out of stuff I would've tossed.

Last week it turned: half a butternut squash, a can of chickpeas, and some wilting spinach into a curry I'd make again.

Not sponsored, just actually use it. Anyone else doing something similar to cut food waste?

One habit that saved me in a tenant dispute last year 👇

When my tenant claimed I "never fixed" the leaking faucet she complained about in October, I had a timestamp, her exact words, and the date I sent the plumber — all in one place.

Here's what most landlords skip: document every maintenance request the moment it comes in. Not in your texts. Not in your head. Somewhere with a timestamp you can actually pull up.

My process now:
• Every unit has its own link (I text it to tenants on move-in day)
• They submit requests through it — no account needed on their end
• I get a notification, it logs automatically, and I can see everything by unit

I've used it twice in 12 months to defend myself against "you never responded" complaints. Both times, the record was right there.

Anyone else learned this the hard way? 👇

Real talk: the hardest part of cooking isn't the cooking. It's deciding what to make at 5:30pm when you've already made 400 decisions today and everyone's hungry.

I started using an AI tool (pantrymate.net) that does one thing: I type what's in my fridge and it tells me what I can make RIGHT NOW. No recipe browsing, no ingredient list planning, no "honey what do you want for dinner" spiral.

It's fast. Like, 30 seconds fast.

Sharing because I know this group is full of people managing family dinners on real schedules. What's your go-to when you're stuck?

Real talk for a second.

2:47 AM. My phone buzzes. It's my tenant at unit 3.

"Hey sorry to bother you but the toilet is making a weird noise and water is coming out the bottom a little"

I'm half asleep, I panic, I send three voice memos, I can't remember if I already dealt with this exact toilet last spring...

Turns out it was fine. Old wax ring. $120 fix. But I'd been awake for two hours because I had no system.

After that I set up a simple maintenance request form for each of my units. Now when something happens, I wake up to a proper request with photos already attached, not a stress-inducing text.

The tool I use is called UnitFix (unitfix.app). Free for one unit, $29/month if you have more. No app for tenants to download.

Anyone else have a "that's when I finally got organized" moment?

Made this last night from stuff I almost forgot I had:

Chicken thighs + half an onion + garlic + a can of diced tomatoes + dried oregano = one-pan baked chicken that took 35 mins.

I typed my ingredients into PantryMate (pantrymate.net) and it suggested this. I was skeptical but it was genuinely good. Now I check it before I grocery shop so I don't buy duplicates of things I already have.

Anyone else doing pantry-first cooking? What did you make recently from random stuff?

Problem: You're not a property manager. But your tenants treat you like one.

When you own 1–3 rental units and self-manage, you don't have a maintenance team. You're the landlord AND the handyman AND the customer service rep AND the accountant.

So when 3 different tenants have 3 different issues this month, you're tracking it all in:
• Your texts
• Your email
• Your memory
• Maybe a note on your fridge

The thing that actually helped me: giving each unit its own maintenance request URL.

Tenant submits a request with a description and photos → I get notified → I can see everything in one dashboard by unit.

Tool is called UnitFix (unitfix.app). Free for 1 unit. $29/month for up to 5. Tenants don't need to create an account or download anything.

If you're managing more than one unit without a system like this, you're doing it harder than it needs to be.

Quick poll for the landlords in here:

When a tenant has a maintenance issue, how do they usually reach you?

📱 Text/WhatsApp
📧 Email
📞 Phone call
🚪 Knock on my door (if you're on-site)
📋 Some kind of form or system

I'm asking because I've been thinking about how much time I lose just triaging tenant messages. Sometimes it's a true emergency. Sometimes it's "the light in the hallway is dim." Both come in the same way — as a text — and I have to figure out urgency while I'm in the middle of something else.

Curious how other small landlords handle this.

For the small landlords who've been winging it — this one's for you.

I built a tool called UnitFix (unitfix.app) specifically for landlords with 1–5 units who self-manage.

Here's what it does:
✅ Each of your rental units gets its own public URL
✅ Tenants submit maintenance requests through it — no account, no app download
✅ You see every request in one dashboard, organized by unit

Right now there's a lifetime deal running — $79 one time for full access (up to 5 units). No subscription, no annual renewals.

If you know a landlord who's still managing maintenance via text thread — tag them. This is exactly for them. 🙏

I'm building an AI startup from Namibia. My MRR right now is $14.

I'm not posting this for sympathy. I'm posting it because I think there's something off about how "building in public" usually gets framed — all milestones, no in-between.

The in-between is: one subscriber. A to-do list that's longer than my runway. An internet connection that drops every time it rains.

The product is PantryMate — you type what's in your fridge, AI tells you what you can cook right now. Simple problem, takes 30 seconds to solve. Google Play launch coming this week.

I built it because I kept staring into my fridge, closing it, and ordering food I didn't need. I figured other people did the same.

Turns out: they do.

If you know someone who'd use it, send them to pantrymate.net. And if you've ever shipped something with ugly early numbers and kept going anyway — I'd like to hear how you thought about it.

PantryMate just launched on Google Play.

The pitch is simple: you type what's in your fridge, the AI tells you what you can cook right now. No meal planning, no grocery list — just what you have, right now.

Built this as a solo founder to solve a genuinely annoying daily problem. Web version has been live for a while. Android is finally here.

If you're on Android and have ever stared into a full fridge with no plan: this is for you.

→ pantrymate.net

Would love any feedback from first users.

I own a few rental units. I'm not a real estate mogul. I'm just someone who bought a duplex and figured out how to manage it without losing my mind.

The part I couldn't figure out: maintenance requests.

They came in by text, at midnight, with no context. I had no way to track what was open, what was resolved, what I'd already dealt with three months ago for the same unit. One time a tenant claimed I never responded to something — and I had no proof either way.

So I built UnitFix.

Each unit gets a public URL. Tenants submit requests without creating an account. I see everything in one dashboard.

That's it. Deliberately boring. Exactly what I needed.

It's live at unitfix.app — free for 1 unit, $29/month for up to 5. I'm also running a one-time lifetime deal right now for early supporters.

If you own 1–5 rentals and still manage maintenance through your texts, this is for you.

The lifetime deal for UnitFix is live.

If you've been curious about it but didn't want to commit to a subscription — this is your window.

$79, one time. Full access for up to 5 rental units. No monthly charges, no annual renewals.

Here's what you get:
→ A unique public URL for each of your units
→ Tenants can submit maintenance requests without creating an account
→ One dashboard showing all open and resolved requests across your properties
→ Photo attachments so you actually know what you're walking into

Built specifically for small landlords (1–5 units) who self-manage.

Deal link: https://buy.stripe.com/6oU28qcJmgEs9Mj3dibsc01

Independent dental practices miss 30% of incoming calls. Most happen after hours, during procedures, or when the front desk is overwhelmed.

And the cost is real: 78% of patients book with the first practice that answers. At an average patient value of $1,500, missing 4 calls a week is $6,000+ in lost monthly revenue.

SmartBook AI is an AI phone receptionist that answers every call, 24/7. It books appointments, handles scheduling questions, and sends SMS confirmations — without adding headcount.

$497/month flat. Less than a part-time hire. Call the live demo yourself: (941) 251-8049

Most dental practice owners I talk to assume they're answering 90%+ of calls.

When we actually track it: it's closer to 70%.

The gap happens:
• During procedures (front desk busy, patient waiting)
• Lunch break

Each missed call = a patient who called the next number on their Google search.

SmartBook AI fills that gap. Answers every call, 24/7, books automatically.

What to Cook With Eggs + Whatever's Left in the Fridge

Eggs are the ultimate flexible ingredient. Combined with almost anything, they make a full meal.

• Eggs + spinach + cheese = frittata (20 min)
• Eggs + rice + soy sauce = fried rice (15 min)
• Eggs + leftover veggies = shakshuka (25 min)

Type YOUR ingredients into PantryMate for a personalized recipe → pantrymate.net

The Lazy Cook's Guide to Zero Food Waste

You don't have to be a meal prep queen to waste less food. You just need a system:

1. Before every grocery run, open your fridge
2. Take a mental (or phone) photo of what's there
3. Type it into PantryMate (takes 60 seconds)
4. Cook one of the suggestions before buying more
